data.title: JPMorgan Leveraging Unparalleled Strength To Build Future Growth
data.description:
When the biggest issue you have with a stock is how to make the valuation work, things are typically going pretty well, and such is the case today for JPMorgan (JPM). Valuation is indeed stretched now relative to banking norms, but given that this bank is in a class by itself with respect to its operating performance, arguments can be made that normal valuation rules don’t, or shouldn't, apply.

Even so, JPMorgan’s share price performance since my last update has been more “average” than exceptional, with the roughly 20% move in the share price since my last update on par for the large bank group as a whole. Over that time, a few stocks like Citi (C), Goldman Sachs (GS), and State Street (STT) have managed even better performances.

Apart from valuation and a little concern about the broader economy, there’s little that concerns me here now. CEO Jamie Dimon will retire at some point, but in the meantime the company has the luxury to reinvest in the business and further widen its performance gap to its peers—the $105B or so that JPMorgan plans to spend next year exceeds the market cap of U.S. Bancorp (USB), the fifth-largest bank by deposits in the United States.

Reinvesting To Widen And Deepen That Moat

Already the largest holder of deposits in the U.S., as well as one of the top players in commercial lending, trading, security services, payments, and asset management, JPMorgan is not content to rest on its laurels and instead continues to reinvest in the growth of the business.

At a recent sell-side conference, the company announced that it was going to spend even more than expected on opex in 2025 – rising opex spending by 9% (about 5% above sell-side estimates), and well ahead of the 1% to 4% spending growth expected for most large U.S. banks in 2026.

While some of this expense growth is tied to structural inflation (everything just costs more) and some to strong current volumes (driving more compensation expense and supporting costs), the company also continues to invest heavily in growing the business – whether that means new branches, new products/services, or underlying core tech investments.

To that end, JPMorgan continues to maintain a massive advantage in its IT spending – while JPMorgan actually spends a little less than average in terms of IT spend as a percentage of revenue (closer to 10% than 12%), the $18B it spends dwarfs most of the industry, with Bank of America (BAC) a distant second at around $13B, Citi at about $12B, and Wells Fargo (WFC) at $11B.

Although it’s true that spending can be wasteful and spending money doesn’t automatically guarantee success, the results do speak for themselves with JPMorgan. Over the past decade, opex spending has grown at an annualized rate of 5% here, while tangible book value per share has increased more than 120%.

Multiple Avenues To Growth

There is no shortage of potential destinations for that spending, and not just in 2025.

Already the largest deposit holder in the U.S., JPMorgan wants to grow its retail deposit share from around 11% to 15%, while also increasing its share in credit cards and connected commerce spending. To help facilitate this, management is planning to add more than 100 branches a year through 2027 while also reinvesting in service quality and product expansion.

While many large banks struggle with Net Promoter Scores (a measure of customer satisfaction), JPMorgan’s score of 44 in the 2024 J.D. Power is easily the best of the big banks, with only Regions (RF) and Citi really in the same neighborhood. This at least partly explains why JPMorgan continues to take share from banks like Bank of America, U.S. Bancorp, Truist, and Wells Fargo across many major markets, and further investments aimed at improving the customer experience should only help.

On the commercial side, the bank is looking to cross-selling as a key opportunity. While the bank is a leading player in investment banking, banking, securities services, and treasury services (including payments), a lot of those businesses have been relatively siloed in the past. Increased IT investments to streamline and simplify service integration should drive improvement here, and the incremental returns on signing up existing customers for existing services that they’re just not currently using is quite high.

There are also long-term opportunities in the asset management and private banking sides of the business. Asset management remains a very fragmented market, as JPMorgan’s 4% share is still good enough for the #5 position, and this is a lucrative cross-selling opportunity for both the consumer and commercial businesses. Private banking is likely to be a more challenging business to grow (currently around 9% share and the #5 position) given the high-touch service requirements, but here again JPMorgan’s huge breadth of services and ability to extend many of those services internationally is a significant asset.

One avenue of growth that seems likely to go largely untapped is M&A. JPMorgan’s sheer size makes acquiring another U.S. bank very challenging from a regulatory perspective, and outside of deposit-gathering businesses, the bank has generally done fine with organic growth efforts (and the failures of the Frank acquisition certainly left a bad taste).

I could see some small fintech deals and perhaps some deals meant to build up asset management, but the real question is whether JPMorgan will look to M&A outside the United States. The bank launched a digital banking business in the U.K. in 2021 and is looking to launch one in Germany next year, but it is at least plausible that the bank could look for a deal. I don’t expect this, particularly given the bank’s past successes in building businesses, but I don’t completely rule it out.

The Outlook

JPMorgan’s third quarter was a little more mixed than the prior quarters in 2025. Revenue was about 3% higher than expected (up 9% yoy and 3% qoq), but net interest income was only up 2% yoy and 3% qoq and missed by a bit. Opex grew less than 8% and beat expectations, and JPMorgan’s 51.5% efficiency ratio was easily the best of its peer group. Pre-provision profits were about 6% better than expected, but some analysts and investors didn’t like the extent to which this was driven by the volatile investment banking and trading operations.

Given the elevated opex spending in FY’26, pre-provision profit growth may only be around 1% to 2% in 2026 ahead of some re-acceleration in FY’27 (around 5% or so). That will make JPMorgan one of the slowest-growing large banks, and it will likewise compress ROTCE expansion some. The Street by and large doesn’t like this, even if it does bear fruit in terms of higher long-term growth rates and ROTCEs down the road.

I’m now looking for around core growth of 3% over the next five years and 4% over the longer term, with significant capital returns to shareholders along the way (in both buybacks and dividends). Discounting those core earnings back, I get a fair value above $300, but not much above. Likewise, premium P/TBV and P/E multiples of 3x and 14x, respectively, get me to around $300 to $320 for fair value.

The Bottom Line

I wouldn’t buy the shares of a poorly run bank just because it looked cheap, and I likewise won’t sell the shares of likely the best-run bank in the world just because they look more or less fairly valued today. Paying fair value for a top-tier asset often works out over the long term, and I’m content to remain a long-term shareholder here. That said, were the Street to start fretting again about the slower pace of growth relative to peer banks and/or were the economy to underperform, it could open another buying window for these shares.

data.title: Black Stone Minerals: A Third Production Agreement Supports An 8% Yield And Haynesville Growth (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
Thesis

Black Stone Minerals (BSM) reported a new production agreement with private equity firm Caturus Energy in the company's Shelby Trough acreage. This deal represents the third Haynesville-based production agreement the company has constructed in addition to the previous agreements with Aethon and Revenant Energy.

Caturus represents a valuable third leg to the company's cash flow generation strategy out of the Haynesville. This deal structures multi-year production growth by phasing in drilling requirements over time.

However, the Caturus deal is unique in the fact that this deal is a first with a vertically integrated producer. Caturus Energy is in the process of developing its own LNG terminal through Commonwealth LNG. This links BSM with a customer with long-term offtake agreements, providing stability in production volumes. This should be welcome news for investors who remember the Aethon time out in 2024.

I am upgrading BSM to a BUY on a long term production and distribution growth thesis.

Third Quarter Results

BSM reported notable production growth in the quarter, with total production up 5% Q-o-Q. This production growth was spread evenly across both oil and natural gas volumes, helping to offset the overall decline in commodity prices. As a result, commodity-based revenue was generally in line with Q2 results.

The third quarter benefited slightly from hedging contracts that allowed the company to report a $6.9 million proceed. Combined, the company reported a distribution coverage of 1.21x, up from 1.18x in the second quarter. This performance exceeded my expectations, as I had previously modeled for a declining production scenario.

As part of the company’s earnings press release, BSM also noted strong near-term future production expectations in both the Permian and in the Haynesville. The company now expects Aethon to turn in line 12 gross wells by early 2026. The timing of these wells is likely structured to capture strong winter demand pulls.

In addition, Coterra (CTRA) is also expected to turn in line 13 Permian wells in Q4. This is expected to be followed by another 16 wells in the first half of 2026. This large pad development should significantly bolster oil production levels throughout 2026.

Cumulatively, the progress in the third quarter production growth combined with the near-term line of sight to a multitude of both natural gas and oil wells bodes well for the upcoming quarters. This dispels most of the concerns I noted in my previous analysis on BSM. In fact, the strong performance likely accelerates my timeline for the company to achieve 1.3x distribution coverage. I had previously not expected this to occur until sometime in the second quarter of 2026.

Long Term Drivers For Distribution Growth

In addition to the near-term development activity, BSM has now developed a robust strategy to facilitate growth on its Haynesville acreage. On December 2nd, the company released the news that it had agreed to a development agreement with Caturus Energy.

This agreement marks the third such deal struck in the Shelby Trough with a private equity producer. However, this deal brings forth a unique aspect to the BSM portfolio. Caturus is also developing a 9.5 MTPA LNG terminal, creating a vertically integrated customer. This helps create a price-agnostic customer who derives profit from both the transportation of the molecules and the spread between US and European hub prices. Henry Hub pricing doesn’t matter as much as contract commitments and the differential between US and international gas prices.

For those investors who still have a few sores from the Ashton timeout in 2024, this should be a welcome diversification in customer base. However, it should be noted that Commonwealth LNG is still far from complete, with the company requesting more time to complete the project.

Turning back to the production agreements, BSM expects at least 23 wells to be drilled between the three companies in 2026. The majority of these wells will still be drilled by Aethon, as this agreement is significantly more mature than the agreements with Revenant and Caturus. These newer agreements will require eight wells to be drilled, with six wells allocated to Revenant Energy and two to Caturus.

Both agreements have a multi-year ramp feature. Revenant Energy’s agreement grows to a minimum of 25 wells annually by 2030. Over a similar period and ending in 2031, Caturus will grow to a 12 well per year minimum, for a total minimum commitment of 53 wells drilled per year by 2031.

These deals also bring a small amount of exploration into the mix. The Caturus and Revenant deals are structured to help BSM delineate and explore its vast acreage. Each company is required to drill pilot and test wells in various areas of interest. This drilling is also required in order to maintain the reduced royalty rate afforded by the production agreements.

Given the recent shift in activity outside of the core Haynesville play, these test wells could prove to be invaluable to BSM over the long run. These wells effectively create an opportunity to have operators bear the operational risks of wildcatting the expansion of both the Haynesville and Bossier shale plays.

Simultaneously, this also provides conformational production data to go along with significant seismic data the company has collected over the last several quarters. Science combined with neighboring production creates a compelling data set for future producers.

Fortunately, the initial reach outside of the core has already been somewhat quantified. As shown in the image above, multiple wells have been drilled in the initial exterior regions of the core with strong production values. Initial reports indicate that as one moves away from the core, the target intervals may be considerably deeper than what is found in traditional Haynesville locations.

This is somewhat concerning for a basin that already must combat high drilling, and completion costs due to significant depths and elevated downhole temperatures. However, the shale thickness in these deeper locations is predicted to be significantly thicker, leading to slower production declines and higher EURs.

In total, BSM expects these deals to culminate in significant production growth. The company expects to grow volumes at a 10% CAGR in line to growing production to over 50 MBOE/d by 2030. The company also expects growth out into 2035, but that is getting a bit too far into the future but could be considered additional upside.

The improved scale will not only improve revenues but also improve operating margin. G&A is the largest expense on BSM’s books. By improving the scale of production, revenues should grow at a faster rate than G&A expenses. This is largely the result of royalty companies requiring generally less employment to grow than an equivalent E&P company.

The net effect is higher distributable cash flow per dollar of generated revenue. As a result, the company expects the distribution growth rate to exceed the rate of revenue growth. Initial projects are to grow the distribution to over $2/unit.

To achieve this, the company is leaning on the efficiencies created by the previously mentioned drilling agreements. The company believes these can lead to higher capital efficiency than in years past. Accordingly, the company expects leverage to stay meaningfully lower than past expansion efforts. To continue to acquire future mineral rights, the company expects the leverage ratio to grow but to be limited to less than 0.6 times EBITDA.

Valuation

Currently, BSM trades slightly over ten times EBITDA but has an improved valuation on a price-to-FCF basis. With the company currently yielding a distribution over 8%, this a reasonable price to pay.

Further support for this statement is the company’s hedge profile. In 2026, the company has approximately 80% of current production hedged with swaps at $3.67/MCF. Further, approximately 60% of oil volumes are hedged at $64.39/barrel. These are highly supportive of future cash and provide significant protection to maintaining the distribution in the near term.

Risks

BSM has aligned itself with three different private equity firms to grow volumes through drilling agreements. Private equity investments are significantly easier to spool up when margins are high. However, these groups are usually also the first to pull capital when margins shrink.

Aethon has been somewhat established as a reliable partner after drilling with BSM for several years. However, Revenant Energy and Caturus Energy are generally newer companies. The current management teams of these companies may be very qualified; however, I have been unable to find any detailed information regarding their operations. In fact, the last time I checked, Revenant Energy’s website was blank and only had 9 employees, according to LinkedIn.

It would be more comforting to have BSM’s growth ambitions tied to more publicly established operators. However, in this case investors will have to trust BSM’s management that they have brokered deals with respectable counterparties. For now, I will assume that BSM scrutinized these arrangements while also learning a thing or two following the Aethon timeout in 2024.

Investor Key Takeaways

1. Production rose 5% in Q3, exceeding my expectations.

2. Both gas and oil volumes are expected to increase due to strong activity via Ashton and Coterra TILs.

3. The company’s growth platform is geared toward the expanding core of the Haynesville. These wells have strong inventories but require high levels of capital investment. In time operators are likely to drive down well costs, supporting future development.

4. The company believes it has solid patterns to achieve a stable 53-well program in the Haynesville by 2030. This would be more than double the current well cadence.

5. BSM trades at a reasonable, although not cheap valuation. The current yield is attractive and supported by a sizable hedge book. BSM is a clear play on future Haynesville production growth.

6. Future prospects and a compelling yield are worthy of a BUY rating.

data.title: Viomi Technology: Access To The U.S. Market To Unlock Sustainable Growth
data.description:
Introduction and Thesis Statement

I am bullish on Viomi Technology Co., Ltd (VIOT), driven by its entry into North America, introducing its MASTER MI smart under-sink water purifier. This purifier has a 9-stage RO filtration system integrated with an AI touchscreen to provide real-time monitoring of volume, water quality, and filter status. I think due to MASTER MI’s capability to remove 99% of contaminants.

Accessing the US market at a time when 64% of Americans have concerns about tap water and only 9% have no concern at all, this shows an existing gap in terms of offering a purification solution. The US water purification market is estimated to grow from $6.8 billion in 2024 to $11.0 billion in 2032. Already, the market penetration of the US water purification market is about 50%, meaning the other half is up for grabs.

Looking at the MASTER MI 9-stage RO filtration system in this US market, I think that is why it has achieved a strong market entry by being in the Top 19 in Amazon’s US water purifiers category by sales volume. Under the sink tankless RO purifier category, the company has attained position 4, which is the highest rank in the history of VIOMI on the Amazon platform. I believe that with the competitive products, which have seen an impressive market entry like in the US, the leverage that has kept its YTD price return at 57.63% beating the S&P 500’s 17.09%.

I will now dive deeper into why I think accessing the US market warrants a buy rating for Viomi Technology.

Company Brief

Viomi Technology Co., Ltd, develops and sells IoT-enabled smart home products through its subsidiaries. The company has a portfolio that includes smart water purification systems, kitchen appliances, and water heaters. The company also sells a wide range of smart devices, such as washing machines, water quality meters, interactive screen TVs, and blenders, among others. Viomi sells complementary consumables like portable fans and speakers directly and through online platforms like Tmall, JD.com, Youpin, and Pinduoduo. It was incorporated in 2014, and it is headquartered in Guangzhou, China.

The Bigger Picture

Before I get to the specifics of MASTER MI smart under-sink purifier, let’s dive into why I think market entry into the US shows potential revenue growth. I introduced that there are 64% of Americans concerned about their tap water, and only 9% have no concerns. Following this concern, 68% of Americans state that the major reason why they are concerned, and use filtration is to remove harmful contaminants, followed by taste improvement. There are other motivators, but what stands out here is that there is a concern about tap water, and this is what motivates Americans to filter their water.

At 64%, concerns about harmful contaminants explain why today 73 million Americans are exposed to Per- and Polyfluoroalkyl Substrates [PFAS]. This research also highlights that in 98% of the sites sampled, there is at least one type of PFAS after frequent tests that surpass federal drinking water levels. These kinds of exposures increase risks related to thyroid disease, cancers, immune system problems, and liver damage, among other health problems.

Owing to my explanation of the market above, I take it as a gap that needs to be filled by new and innovative water purifiers in the US market. From a global perspective, water purification is growing at a CAGR of 7.44% between 2024 and $36.5 million to $64.5 million in 2033. China will take a larger market share, from $6.2 billion in 2024 to $12.4 billion, which is understandable as its market penetration is still at an early stage of 28%. Compared to the US, which is 50% penetrated, the remaining market share is up for capture.

This is why I believe that the 2026 flagship of MASTER MI AI Alkaline under-sink water purifier will serve the US market. Given its impressive market entry in the US, this shows that Viomi Technology water purifiers have a better technological capability compared to peer brands. Now it has secured around 1,8467 patents in water purification technology and 690 invention patents.

You see, with a production capacity of 5 million water purifiers and 30 million filter elements, the company has the capacity to produce water purifiers for the North American market. The product (MASTER M1 Alkaline water purifier) is specifically targeted to solve the problem of quality water relating to PFAS contamination in North America. Already, the signs of a good market acceptance have started showing a positive side by being in the Top 19 in the Amazon US water purifier category.

Financials & Valuation

Viomi’s top-line was not doing well between 2021 and 2023, dipping from $892.4 million in 2020 to a low of $230.9 million in 2023. This was primarily due to sluggish demand in China, which affected home appliances and water purification systems. However, China continues to boost its consumer spending, and I believe this is also contributing to a revenue rebound in Viomi. For instance, the trailing revenue is currently at $385.3 million, representing a 23.81% YoY. The good thing about Viomi is that, according to the consensus revenue estimate for FY2025, the revenues might hit $415.07 million, representing 42.15% YoY growth. As China's buying power continues to increase, and considering the entry into the US market is still in early stages, the revenues in 2026 have a modest increase to $419.07 million, representing 0.96% YoY growth. Nevertheless, I expect this to improve in 2027 and beyond as the company keeps expanding in the US market. As such, I believe a growth rate averaging 15% is likely alongside the projected US Consumer Discretionary sector growth of about 14%.

Moving to profitability, VIOT is revealing to have a strong execution with a trailing net income of $24.8 million up from $8.7 million in 2024 representing more than 180% growth. Viomi’s debut on Amazon during Black Friday/Cyber Monday, which saw Viomi products achieve Top 19 in the water purifier category, is a sign of good acceptance. Owing to this optimism, I think this is a major reason behind the consensus EPS estimates of $0.45 in FY2025, representing 254.81% and is likely to maintain the same $0.45 as it approaches new markets such as the US market. The interplay here is the expected high revenue growth against what appears to be a relatively stable operating expenses.

As I conclude the section, I will value VIOT using the P/S multiple comparatively to that of its competitors, Haier Smart Home Co., Ltd (HRSHF) and Panasonic Holdings Corporation (PCRFF). The P/S multiple of VIOT is 0.41x, and compared to its peers, HRSHF at 0.81x and PCRFF at 0.56x, it indicates that the market has undervalued VIOT.

When I look at the trailing growth rates, I see VIOT as a value opportunity. This is because with a trailing YoY revenue growth of 23.81%, none of these peers is close to it. The nearest is HRSHF at 6.55% and PCRFF lags at -6.97%, both averaging -0.21% despite the average P/S of 0.69x being 1.7x that of VIOT. This is a clear undervaluation which I believe the market should seize. While I expect this company to sustain a growth rate in the north of 10% past 2026 due to its new market expansion, I expect a market revaluation. Assuming a target P/S of 0.7x which is the average for the peers, and the consensus revenue of $415.07 for 2025, I estimate a price target of $4.28.

Investment Risks

High SG&A Burn Rate Tied To US Expansion

The Selling General and Administration costs are trailing at $48.3 million, up from $38.6 million in 2024. This means that although the company is having a good market acceptance, it is at an increasing cost. This indicates the company is spending more on logistics and marketing in the US entry. In case the sales fail to pick up as intended, this may affect scale proportionally, eroding margins.

Exposure to U.S.-China Trade Tension

The US-China trade tensions are still ongoing, and China’s manufacturing and supply chain is likely to affect water purifiers. Material risk is one of the areas facing regulatory barriers. This could raise the cost and price competitiveness of its water purifiers in the American market, which could reduce the revenues. With initiatives such as Black Friday recording success, it also reveals there is an existing demand, but with the prolonged geopolitical friction, this may erode the margins or disrupt logistics and financial forecasts.

Conclusion

I believe that early market acceptance in the US market, at a time it is facing a purified water challenge, has an upside reversal pattern. This is why I am reiterating my bullish stance, hence my buy rating.
